A team builds a system meant to produce discoveries by generating verbal hypotheses and criticizing them in language. It works best when success can be expressed as finding the right explanation. But one stated edge case already limits it: some domains do not need a better explanation first; they need a new way to measure, or a new experimental protocol. In those domains, language can describe the need, but not supply the crucial step if the step is procedural or instrumental.

A second premise deepens the problem. The journal case looks convincing partly because it retells a long, uneven history as if it were a clean arc. If that persuasive neatness is mistaken for real explanatory discovery, then the architecture may be judged by stories that only look like discovery after compression. The apparent success condition becomes suspicious: perhaps the system is good at producing retrospective coherence rather than the original work that made later coherence possible.

Einstein’s case sharpens this into a structural objection. If his route from an initial intuition to field equations required ten years of mathematics, then a mostly verbal generate-and-criticize loop may miss the very medium in which progress had to occur. The system could perhaps talk about the problem, talk about candidate principles, and talk about failures. But if the actual advance required sustained mathematical construction, then verbal search is not just inefficient; it may be operating in the wrong space.

So the architecture faces at least two mismatches: some discoveries require new measurements rather than new descriptions, and some require mathematical development rather than verbal iteration. In both cases, narrative success can hide operational failure. A polished account of “how the system discovered X” may therefore be strongest exactly where one should distrust it most.

The remaining two premises introduce agents whose behavior is defined less by explanation than by disposition and restraint. The generals admired the supermarket on the wonder islands for its willingness. Whatever else that means, admiration is directed not at correctness or theory but at readiness or compliance. The guitarist, though diseased and smarter, chose adjournment over cheating. That choice privileges procedure over opportunistic success.

Placed beside the architecture, these figures mark two possible evaluation cultures. One culture admires willingness: produce answers, keep the process moving, supply a neat account. The other accepts adjournment rather than cheating: if the needed measurement does not exist, or the mathematics has not been done, one pauses instead of pretending language has closed the gap.

From the premises together, the safest world-model is this: a language-centered discovery architecture can be highly persuasive while failing at the points where real novelty depends on nonlinguistic construction. If evaluators are like the generals, they may reward willingness and coherence. If they are like the guitarist, they will prefer delay to counterfeit discovery. The difference determines whether the system is treated as a discoverer or as a narrator of discoveries it has not actually made.
